label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. The article is about a study where students used either first-person or second-person self-talk to motivate themselves before a task. The question is asking how the students performed in the experiment based on the study's findings. Let me read through the details again to make sure I get it right. The study had 143 undergraduates. Half wrote advice in first-person ("I can do this") and the other half in second-person ("You can do this"). The results? The second-person group completed more puzzles and had a more positive attitude. Also, when it came to exercise advice, the second-person group was more positive and planned to exercise more. The options are A, B, C, D. Let's look at each choice. Option A says first-person students had a better attitude. But the article says the second-person group had a more positive attitude, so A is incorrect. Option B says those who talked to themselves completed the most puzzles. However, the experiment had them writing, not talking aloud. The article mentions they wrote down advice. Also, the correct group was the second-person writers, not necessarily those who talked. Plus, the study didn't compare talking vs writing, just the pronouns used in writing. So B is not
